title: Metric and manifold repair for missing data
speaker: Anna Gilbert
tags: Virtual Workshop on Missing Data Challenges in Computation Statistics and Applications

proof_stages.label: motivation from corrupted data geometry
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates metric repair by arguing that missing or corrupted data can distort pairwise distances and thereby damage downstream machine-learning and optimization tasks that rely on metric structure.

proof_stages.label: formal sparse metric repair problem
proof_stages.summary: A distance matrix is encoded by triangle-inequality linear constraints, metric repair is formulated as finding a perturbation making a corrupted matrix metric, and the desired version is sparse repair minimizing the number of changed distances rather than a dense nearest-metric projection.

proof_stages.label: algorithmic barrier and APSP connection
proof_stages.summary: Known conditional lower bounds of Williams and Williams indicate that even checking whether a matrix is metric is unlikely to beat cubic time, while also pointing to a structural connection with all-pairs shortest paths.

proof_stages.label: decrease-only repair by Floyd-Warshall
proof_stages.summary: After separating decrease-only, increase-only, and unrestricted perturbations, the decrease-only problem is solved exactly by running Floyd-Warshall/all-pairs shortest paths, and the resulting perturbation is shown to be sparsest and closest in every �lp norm.

proof_stages.label: hardness and graph metric repair
proof_stages.summary: The tractability of decrease-only repair is contrasted with the NP-completeness and approximation challenges of allowing positive increases, and the framework is generalized from complete-graph triangle inequalities to weighted graph cycle repair.

proof_stages.label: manifold repair for missing-data embeddings
proof_stages.summary: Sparse metric repair is inserted into the standard manifold-learning pipeline so that corrupted distance matrices from missing data are repaired before algorithms such as Isomap produce low-dimensional embeddings.

proof_stages.label: metric-structure learning before embedding
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by advocating a two-step philosophy: first learn or repair the combinatorial metric/geometric structure of the data, then embed it, with remaining open questions about uniqueness, stability, Euclidean distance matrices, and rank behavior.

examples.name: single-cell RNA-seq sparse expression matrices
examples.note: Sparse cell-by-gene count matrices, with 90--95% zero entries, are presented as the motivating data source that led the speaker to ask how broken distance geometry affects downstream learning without directly imputing the data.
examples.engagement_reason: she uses single-cell RNA-seq matrices as motivation, describing their sparsity and distorted distances without repairing or computing on a specific dataset

examples.name: the 1-2-7 broken triangle
examples.note: The triangle with edge lengths 1, 2, and 7 shows that decrease-only repair changes the long edge to 3 whereas increase-only repair changes a short edge to 5, visibly producing the sign-restricted case split and later illustrating lack of consistency between the two repairs.
examples.engagement_reason: she explicitly analyzes the 1-2-7 triangle, decreasing 7 to 3 in one case and increasing 1 to 5 in another

passes.reason: the 1-2-7 weighted triangle is used to show decrease-only and increase-only repairs need not agree

examples.name: long-edge and epsilon-edge broken cycles
examples.note: Weighted cycles with one very long edge, or with a tiny epsilon edge that must be increased, make concrete the passage from triangle inequalities to graph-cycle inequalities and the difficulty of choosing increase-only repairs.
examples.engagement_reason: she draws and reasons through broken graph cycles, including a long edge of 10 and an epsilon edge whose increase repairs the violation

passes.reason: the broken cycles use very long or epsilon-small edge weights as adversarial witnesses to graph-metric repair difficulty
broken-cycle gadgets with a dominating long edge or near-zero epsilon edge expose fragile increase-only repair choices.
the broken-cycle gadgets, especially the near-zero epsilon edge, are limiting adversarial cases showing increase-only repair choices can fail.
The broken cycles, especially the nearly collapsed epsilon edge, serve as adversarial obstructions for graph metric repair.
the cycles use a dominating long edge or arbitrarily small epsilon edge as adversarial broken-cycle cases
the broken-cycle gadgets include a near-zero epsilon edge and are used as adversarial examples for increase-only repair
the cycles with a dominating long edge or an epsilon edge illustrate adversarial broken-cycle behavior, with the epsilon/long edges forming limiting extreme weights
The broken cycles, especially the almost-zero epsilon edge with a dominating violation, serve as limiting enemy cases for naive increase-only repair.
The broken cycles are adversarial metric-obstruction gadgets, with the epsilon-edge case giving a near-collapsed limiting edge.

examples.name: Swiss-roll Isomap repair experiment
examples.note: A Swiss-roll dataset with randomly missing coordinates demonstrates that Isomap on corrupted distances collapses the embedding, while repairing the distance matrix before Isomap recovers a much more plausible unrolling.
examples.engagement_reason: she describes running an Isomap experiment on a Swiss roll before corruption, after coordinate deletion, and after metric repair, comparing the resulting embeddings

passes.reason: the Swiss-roll experiment shows the uncorrected Isomap pipeline can fail under missing-coordinate distance corruption
